title: Listening to the ocean since 2006
text:
Overview
Ocean Networks Canada has been recording the underwater soundscape since the first installation of the VENUS node in Saanich Inlet in 2006. What started as a single location has expanded into a pan-Canadian array monitoring the Pacific, Atlantic, and Arctic coasts.
As of June 04, 2026, ONC manages 36 active hydrophones from coast to coast to coast.





Hydrophone Network at Ocean Networks Canada. Most hydrophones are located on the west coast. There is currently one in the Arctic, and one in the east coast.



This handbook is an internal source for ONC’s hydrophone network, spanning hardware specs, signal processing, and the lifecycle of an instrument.


Why listening to the ocean?
Sound is the primary way we “see” in the ocean. Because light travels poorly in water, acoustic signals are essential for navigation, communication, and monitoring the health of marine ecosystems. Unlike several oceanic instruments, hydrophones use passive acoustics, hence are a non-disruptive form of monitoring the ocean soundscape.






Hydrophone under water.





From national defense using SOSUS (sound surveillance system) to identify vessels and nuclear testing, to resource exploration using seismic surveys to map minerals, acoustics drive the blue economy. These sensors can also be used to monitor vessel traffic, which for example is increasing in the Arctic as sea ice melts unveiling new shipping routes.
Hydrophones are also used to track marine mammals distribution and abundance. Beyond biology, hydrophones can be used to detect as earthquakes and seismic activity, and even the sound of wind and rain


Goals and future direction for passive acoustics

Delivering science-ready (trusted) hydrophone data and data products
Becoming a reference in terms of passive acoustics data quality assessment and calibration
Leader in Arctic hydrophone array deployments



Territory Acknowledgment
We recognize that marine research in the Victoria region takes place on the traditional lands of the Lək̓ʷəŋən (Lekwungen) people, known today as the Songhees and Esquimalt Nations, as well as the Xwsepsum (Esquimalt) and W̱SÁNEĆ peoples.

title: Hydrophone Life Cycle
text:
This section outlines the end-to-end journey of a hydrophone at ONC, from the initial purchase and contract to long-term data analysis. Thie life cycle is based on Aghaei et al., 2024: Best Practices for Managing Passive Acoustics Data at Ocean Networks Canada.
Hydrophones have been in use at ONC since 2006, totalling more than 700 TB of archived data.
The life cycle workflow ensures that data is not just collected, but are FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable). ONC commits to share and preserve data long-term.



Diagram of a Hydrophone life cycle at Ocean Networks Canada.



1. Planning and Governance
Teams involved: Science, DAQ, DSO, Engineers
Before a hydrophone is deployed in the water, the following legal and technical steps must be taken:

Data Agreements: ONC establishes legal agreements to define data ownership, usage restrictions, and attribution.
Stewardship: Data Stewards (DSO) apply standardized metadata and attributions to ensure the sensor is discoverable.
Product Definition: We identify new data products the community might need.



2. Data Collection & Signal Chain
Teams involved: DAQ, DSO, Systems, Marine Ops, Engineers

Hardware: We primarily use Ocean Sonics (HF/AF) and GeoSpectrum (from JASCO distributor) instruments, deployed as single units or four-element arrays.
Calibration (HydroCal): Instruments undergo rigorous pre-deployment and post-recovery calibration to ensure accuracy. If sensors drift, they are returned to the manufacturer.
Data Diversion: Hydrophones are “Tier 1” instruments, meaning that the data streams are subject to diversion by the Royal Canadian Navy and US Navy before reaching the ONC archive.
Data Transmission:

Acoustic Data: Transferred as raw audio (.wav or .flac).
Ancillary Data: Instrument “health” (temperature, humidity, clock drift) is transmitted without diversion to minitor device’s status (only for Ocean Sonics). Also referred to as auxiliary data.

Data Processing: Performed to generate Spectral Data (HSD) in MAT format, and Spectral Density Plots (HSDP) the raw audio files, and archived.
Data Availability: Data is made readily available (within a few minutes) to end-users through Oceans 3.0.



3. Commissioning
Teams involved: DAQ, DSO
Once a hydrophone is deployed and recording data, the commissioning of the device starts. This process is designed to catch technical issues early and ensures the data is “science-ready”.

Immediate Post-Deployment: Immediately after deployment, we confirm the instrument is able to record and send data to ONC archive. A public annotation is created in Oceans 3.0 to mark the start of the data streaming. A JIRA ticket is created which consolidates all relevant information.
1-2 Months Post-Deployment: A detailed analysis of data stream is performed, and several elements are analyzed including the presence of noise, saturation, array consistency (for 4 elements arrays), auxiliary data.

The commissioning ticket is closed one the data is confirmed found in the Hydrophone Data Viewer, accessible through ONC API, and that the data products are generated correctly.
For the complete procedure: Hydrophone Commissioning Procedure


4. Data Quality & Assurance
Teams involved: DAQ, Science, Systems
Because hydrophone data is classified as complex data (unlike scalar data), standard automated QAQC (like those for temperature or salinity) does not yet exist.

Manual Inspection: The DAQ team performs daily visual checks for issues like clipping, electronic interference, energy spikes, sensitivity drifts.
Annotations: Any unusual events or data gaps (caused by network maintenance, instrument failure, communication down) are recorded as annotations in Oceans 3.0 so researchers know why data might be missing.



5. Metadata & Standardization
Teams involved: DSO
To make our data globally compatible, we follow strict international standards:

ISO 19115: Governs our high-level metadata records
Global Catalogues: We align with IOOS, SeaDataNet and SeaVox device categories.
FAIR Principles: Ensuring every byte of data is Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable.
Device Action: Every event in a hydrophone life cycle, from deplyoment, calibrations, to recovery, is recorded as a permanent action in the database.



6. Preservation & Storage
Teams involved: Software, DSO, Systems

Primary Storage: Terabytes of data are stored locally at the University of Victoria data center.
Redundancy: A complete offsite backup is maintained to prevent data loss, and also shares data processing load.



7. Access & Analysis

Teams involved: Science, DSO, End-Users*
Discovery: Users find data via Oceans 3.0 using Data Search, the Hydrophone Viewer (5-minute spectrogram previews), or the API.
Identification: Each dataset is assigned a DOI (Digital Object Identifier) to ensure researchers can cite the specific data they used in their papers.



8. Recovery & Decommissioning
Teams involved: DSO, MarineOps, Systems
The end of a deployement does not mean the end of a instrument’s use. Hydrophones are often re-deployed during the ONC maintenance cruises, or returning to MTC of the manufacturer for maintenance.

Post-Recovery Calibration: Once a hydrophone is pulled out of the water, calibration is performed to check if sensitivity shifted dramatically while underwater. If the before and after are very different, the instrument is often sent back to the manufacturer.
Re-Deployment: Hydrophones are often re-deployed at the same location, or at a new site. If moved to a different site, the instrument keeps its device code, but the device id will be re-assigned.
Permanent Decommissioning (Goodbye): When an instrument reaches the end of its functional life or can no longer be repaired, it is officially decommissioned in the database.

title: How do hydrophones work?
text:
General
A hydrophone is a device that passively detects acoustic signal underwater. The word hydrophone comes from the Greek hydro (water), and phone (voice).
Acoustic data are recorded in the form of a time-amplitude signal, at a specified sampling rate.
Several types of hydrophones exist depending on the applications, with different frequency range. Some of the most common applications of passive acoustics are:

title: How do hydrophones work?
text:
Signal to Noise ratio
Signal to Noise ratio is an important element to consider for hydrophones.
A bigger element increases sensitivity, but also can lower SNR.
A rule of thumb is to ensure the measured amplitude is at least 5 times larger than the noise equavalent pressure (NEP) of the hydrophone to maximize the signal to noise ratio (SNR).
\[
NEP [Pa] = \frac{\text{Pre-amp Noise} ~[\mu V]}{\text{Sensitivity} ~[nV~Pa^{-1}] \times \text{Pre-amp Gain}} \times 1000
\]

title: Sound Propagation in the Ocean
text:
What is sound?
In the ocean, light attenuates rapidly, whereas sound can travel thousands of kilometers, making sound is the primary sense for communication and navigation for marine mammals and human underwater telecommunications.
Sound is created by a vibrating object which compressesion and expansion produces a pressure difference which generates a sound wave that propagates. The resulting sound wave is a longitudinal oscillatory wave, which travels through a medium (e.g., water, air) as alternating high and low pressures. As sound propagates under water, it induces lateral movement of water molecules which creates pressure change. The closer the molecules, the tighter their bonds, the less time it takes to pass the sound to each other.
Note that a sound wave is omnidirectional, meaning that it propagates in all directions.
crumbs: 4  Sound Propagation in the Ocean

title: Sound Propagation in the Ocean
text:
Sound characterization
Sound is described by characteristics that can be measured:

Amplitude & Intensity (Loudness): Amplitude represents the energy the wave carries, and how much the water particles move. Intensity is the energy transmitted through a unit area per time in the direction of travel. Relative intensity is expressed in decibels (dB).
Frequency (\(f\)) (Pitch): Measured in Hertz (Hz). Frequency defines the sound’s pitch by how often the water particles oscillate. In general, larger objects (like baleen whales or container ships) produce lower frequency sounds, while smaller objects produce higher frequency sounds.




Bigger objects make low-frequency sounds while smaller objects make high frequency sounds.



Wavelength (\(\lambda\)): The distance over which the wave’s shape repeats, directly related to the speed of sound (\(c\)) and frequency (\(f\)): \(c=f\lambda\).
Phase (\(\phi\)): Phase specifies the exact alignment or timing of a wave at a given moment. While it cannot be heard, phase is critical for hydrophone arrays to determine the precise direction a sound is coming from.

Mathematical representation of a classic pressure (\(p\)) sine wave as a function of time (\(t\)): \[
p(t) = A \sin{(2\pi f t + \phi)}
\]
crumbs: 4  Sound Propagation in the Ocean

title: Sound Propagation in the Ocean
section: Why does sound travel faster in water than air?
text:
Why does sound travel faster in water than air?
Sound travels roughly 4.3 times faster in water than in air (\(\approx 1500\) m/s in water vs 340 m/s in air). he speed of sound (\(v\)) is governed by the relationship between a material’s bulk modulus (B), its resistance to compression) and its density (\(\rho\)):


Bulk Modulus: The stiffness, or resistance to compression of a medium, which depends on the medium’s density: \(B=\rho \partial P / \partial \rho\).
\[
v = \sqrt{\left(B/\rho\right)}
\]
While water is 800 times denser than air (1000 kg/m vs 1.2 kg m/3), it is also 20,000 times less compressible than air. The extreme stiffness of seawater allows for kinetic energy to transfer more efficienttly between molecules and as a result, sound to travel much fast underwater.
crumbs: 4  Sound Propagation in the Ocean

title: Sound Propagation in the Ocean
text:
SOFAR channel (ocean acoustic waveguide)
There’s a unique sweet spot at about 1000 m where sound emitted at this depth can travel much farther away, known as the SOFAR channel (SOund Fixing And Ranging). This is a result of decrease in temperature and the increase in pressure making sound speed reach a miniumum. Because of refraction, sound waves always bend toward regions of slower speed. As a result, any sound waves traveling upward or downward from this 1000-meter layer are continuously bended right back into the center of the low-speed layer.


SOFAR channel: The specific depth range at which the speed of sound reaches a minimum, usually around 1000 m.

Refraction: The bending of a wave as it moves through changing water properties (temperature, pressure, salinity) Sound will bend from high sound speed to low sound speed (Snell’s law).


This deep channel is also called an ocean waveguide, where inside acoustic energy does not escape and thus can travel great distances. This channel has several useful applications:

Marine mammals use it for communications with low-frequency sounds across thousands of kilometers.
During WWII, the military used it to locate downed aviators, which would released small detonations within the SOFAR channel detectable far away.
crumbs: 4  Sound Propagation in the Ocean

title: Sound Propagation in the Ocean
text:
Surface Duct
Similar to the SOFAR Channel, the Duct surface channel is a channel where sound gets trapped, within *** of the surface. This is due to …
Surface sound channel where all sound waves bend upwards.



Model simulation of sound trapped in a surface duct. Figure from Lynch et al., 2018; Fig. 5.
crumbs: 4  Sound Propagation in the Ocean

title: Sound Propagation in the Ocean
text:
Absorption
As sound travels through water, it gets absorbed, caught by the molecules which vibrate and transform the wave energy into heat. As a result, the sound wave amplitude decreases as it propagates. High frequency sound gets absorbed faster, so high frequency sounds do not travel as far as low frequency sounds.
crumbs: 4  Sound Propagation in the Ocean

title: Sound Propagation in the Ocean
text:
Attenuation
A sound wave is spherical, with propagation in all directions. As it propagates, the aplitude (or intensity) is reduces (~ \(1/r^2\)) due to absorption or scattering within the surrounding medium (sea water). Attenuation is very small for low frequencies, so sounds like doplhin clicks are absorbed rapidly and vanish, while whale calls or ships rumbles can propagate across entire ocean basins with minimal energy loss.
crumbs: 4  Sound Propagation in the Ocean

title: Sound Propagation in the Ocean
text:
Diffraction
Diffraction occurs when a sound wave is partially obstructed. The rays behind a wall (or sea mount) are reflected.
crumbs: 4  Sound Propagation in the Ocean

title: Sound Propagation in the Ocean
text:
Reflection
Sound waves reflect and bounce off a surface (e.g., sea surface, sea floor), creating echoes. Elements like sea floor sediment types can impact reflection efficiency.
A seafloor of hard bedrock and is a better reflector than watery sand.



Reflection of a sound wave as it reaches the surface. Figure from Lynch et al., 2018; Fig. 2.
crumbs: 4  Sound Propagation in the Ocean

title: Sound Propagation in the Ocean
text:
Interference
Multiple paths can create constructive and destructive interference. When a sound is emitted and propagates, the sound waves take different paths from reflecting and bouncing around. They arrive at the same hydrophone at slightly different times, causing their waveforms to overlap and create interference.

constructive interference: two peaks arrive exactly at the same time, reinforcing their intensity and creating an artificially loud sound.
destructive interference: the trough of one aligns with the peak of the other, annulling the signal which can make the sound completely disappear.




Constructive and destructive interference.
crumbs: 4  Sound Propagation in the Ocean

title: Sound Propagation in the Ocean
text:
Refraction
Refraction is the change of direction (also bending) of sound propagation, due to changes in the sound speed from changes in temperature, salinity, or pressure.



Example of refracted ray paths. Credits: Kessel & Hollett, 2006


The refraction angle (direction) follows Snell’s Law:
\[
\frac{\sin{\theta_1}}{\sin{\theta_2}} = \frac{c_1}{c_2}
\]
The frequency of the sound wave does not change while being refracted, but its wavelength might depending on the refracted angle and its velocity (\(c=\lambda f\)).
Two wave guides in the ocean are a result of refraction: the SOFAR channel, and the surface duct.
crumbs: 4  Sound Propagation in the Ocean

title: Sound Propagation in the Ocean
text:
Scattering
When a sound wave encouters a boundary (sea surface, sea floor, sea mount, marine animal), the wave is reflected if the surface is smooth. In case of a rough surface or complex medium (bubbles, turbulence, heavy storm, small fishes, suspended sediments), the sound wave is reflected in many directions all at once. This causes the sound to disperse in random direction and the acoustic signal loses energy quickly.
crumbs: 4  Sound Propagation in the Ocean

title: Sound Propagation in the Ocean
text:
Reverberation
Reverberation is not exactly a wave behaviour, but rather the result of it. It is the accumulation of sound that have been scattered and persists even if the source is turned off. It can limit sound detection due to clouding the incoming signal. It is even more important in shallow water, as sound interacts multiple times with the surface and the bottom as it travels.
crumbs: 4  Sound Propagation in the Ocean

title: Sound Propagation in the Ocean
text:
Geometric Spreading (GS)
Geometric spreading, energy loss due to sound spreading in space, is independant of frequency, but it depends on the shape of the spreading:

Deep ocean and open water (spherical spreading): \(GS = 20 \log_{10} (r)\)
Shallow water and surface ducts (cylindrical spreading): \(GS = 10 \log_{10} (r)\)
crumbs: 4  Sound Propagation in the Ocean

title: Sound Propagation in the Ocean
text:
Excess Attenuation (EA)
Energy is lost through reflection, scattering, diffraction depending on environmental conditions, terrain, weather:
\[
EA =  \text{Loss}_{absorption} + \text{Loss}_{reflection} + \text{Loss}_{scattering} + \text{Loss}_{diffraction}
\]

Absorption: \(\alpha * r\), energy is absorbed by seawater and converted to heat.
Boundary reflection and scattering: energy is lost when sound hits the seafloor, the sea surface, or sea ice.
Diffraction: energy is diverted when a wave bends around underwater seamounts or ridges.
crumbs: 4  Sound Propagation in the Ocean

title: Sound Propagation in the Ocean
section: How does sea ice affect how sound travel?
text:
How does sea ice affect how sound travel?
When sea ice is present, the upward bending sound waves constantly interact with the rough sea ice surface which creates constant scattering. Historically, this instance scattering has limited long-range sound propagation to frequencies below 30 Hz. Under global warmin, the Arctic has warmed and sea ice extent has decreased dramatically, affecting how sound travels and making higher frequencies travel longer ranges than before.
crumbs: 4  Sound Propagation in the Ocean

title: Sound Propagation in the Ocean
text:
The polar sound speed profile
The sound profile in polar regions varies differently than in temperate regions. Unlike temperate oceans, polar temperatures are much less variable with depth, which lead to specific arctic sound speed characteristics:

wintertime: sound speed minimum sits at the surface. Because sound speed increases with depth, sound waves are constantly refracted upward towards the surface, causing massive energy loss through intense scattering against sea ice.
summertime: solar heating warms the upper layer, shifting the sound speed minimum, and.sub-surface duct, at around 150 m. This deep channel isolates sound waves from the surface, allowing high frequencies to travel much further.




Typical sound speed profile in the Arctic.


Read more: Ambient Noise in the Canadian Arctic, pp 105-133, from Cook, Barclay & Richards.
crumbs: 4  Sound Propagation in the Ocean

title: Sound Propagation in the Ocean
text:
Decibel (dB)
Underwater sounds span many orders of magnitude, so sound pressure levels are measured on a base-10 logarithmic scale, called the decibel (dB). The standard reference pressure for underwater sounds is 1 \(\mu\) Pa (whereas 20\(\mu\) Pa is the reference for air).
\[
SPL = 20 \log_{10} \left(\frac{p}{p_{ref}}\right)~\text{dB re 1}~\mu\text{Pa}
\]
Example: a sound wave with a pressure of 1,000,000 times greater than the reference pressure has a sound pressure level of: \[
20 \log_{10}\left(10^6\right) = 120~\text{dB re 1}~\mu\text{Pa}
\]
crumbs: 4  Sound Propagation in the Ocean

title: Sound Propagation in the Ocean
section: Spectral units: from space to frequency domain
text:
Spectral units: from space to frequency domain
The raw, continuous audio data is decomposed into individual frequencies using a Fourier Transform. This transformation produces the Power Spectral Density (PSD), expressed in units of \(\text{Pa}^2/\text{Hz}\), which reflects how acoustic energy is distributed across the frequencies.
To make these values easier to read and analyze, we convert the PSD to a logarithmic scale. The resulting Spectrum Level measures the acoustic intensity contained within a 1 Hz bandwidth:



\(\text{Spectrum Level Unit: dB re } 1\ \mu\text{Pa}^2/\text{Hz}\)
crumbs: 4  Sound Propagation in the Ocean

title: Ocean Soundscape & Taxonomy
text:
Taxonomy
A list of sound labels has been compiled under a Taxonomy for Passive Acoustic Data Classification. This taxonomy serves as the foundational label set for our ML anomaly autodetector to automate soundscape classification.



Anthropophony

In-Air Source (Aircraft, Snowmobile)
Industrial Activity (Dredging, Mining, Pile driving)
Sonar (Fisheries, Naval)
Submersible (Human-occupied vehicle, Remotely operated vehicle)
Surveying (Airgun, Explosive)
Vessel (Cargo, Fishing, Icebreaker, Military, Passenger, Pleasure, Sailing, Research, Tanker, Tug)
Unknown Anthropophony

Biophony

Crustacean (Crab, Lobster, Shrimp/Snapping shrimp)
Fish (Vent fish, Fish chorus)
Marine Mammal

Cetacean

Baleen Whales (Bowhead, Blue, Fin, Gray, Humpback, Minke, North Atlantic Right, North Pacific Right, Sei)
Toothed Whales

Beaked (Baird’s, Cuvier’s)
Beluga
Dolphin (Atlantic Spotted, Common Bottlenose, Common, Northern Right Whale, PAcific White-Sided, Risso’s, Striped)
False Killer Whale
Killer Whale/Orcas (Bigg’s, Northern Resident, Southern Resident, Offshore)
Narwhal
Pospoise (Dall’s, Harbour)
Sperm


Pinniped (Seal, Walrus)

Unknown Biophony (Bioacoustic communication signal, Echolocation click, Click train, Drumming, Grinding, Snapping, Stridulation, Vocalization)
Attributes: Song, Moan, Upsweep, Downsweep

Geophony

Environmental (Flow noise, Ice cracking, Iceberg collision, Tsunami)
Geology

Bubbling (Methane seep)
Earthquake
Hydrothermal vent (Chimney collapse, Magma)
Sedimentation
Turbidity current

Weather

Lightning
Precipitation (Rain, Snow, Hail)
Wind
Waves


Attributes: Continuous vs Impulsive

Instrumentation

Hydrophone Contact
Malfunction (Clipping, Data Gap, Frequency dropout, Sensitivity change, Time dropout)
Other ONC Equipment (ADCP, Camera, Mooring noise/Chain noise)
Self-Noise (Acoustic, Non-Acoustic/Tonal)
Unknown Instrumentation





References

title: ONC Hydrophone Network
text:
Ocean Networks Canada operates a massive spatial array of underwater acoustics monitoring across the country. Each hydrophone has a designated Device Code (ex: ICLISTENHF6016) and is located at a designated Location code (ex: CSQH.H1). The Location Code is used by the database, but they also have a Colloquial Name (ex: Clayoquot Slope), used by humans. In addition, there are two observatories VENUS and NEPTUNE, which regroup several sites.
Most locations have a single hydrophone, while some sites have hydrophone arrays, which constitutes several hydrophones mounted together on a platform. The hydrophone arrays naming convention has .H1, .H2, etc., added to the location code, to denote the position of the hydrophone within the array.

Main locations

Pacific: NEPTUNE (offshore) and VENUS (coastal) observatories.
Arctic: Cambridge Bay, Gascoyne Inlet, NU.
Atlantic: Conception Bay, Holyrood, NL.



Map






L = require("leaflet@1.9.4")
html`<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://unpkg.com/leaflet@1.9.4/dist/leaflet.css">`














container = html`<div style="height: 600px; border-radius: 10px; border: 1px solid #ddd;"></div>`
yield container;














{
  const map = L.map(container).setView([58, -95], 3);

  L.tileLayer('https://server.arcgisonline.com/ArcGIS/rest/services/Ocean/World_Ocean_Base/MapServer/tile/{z}/{y}/{x}', {
    attribution: 'Esri & Sources',
    maxZoom: 13
  }).addTo(map);

  // 1. Initialize Layer Groups
  const cableLayer = L.layerGroup().addTo(map);
  const inactiveLayer = L.layerGroup();
  const activeLayer = L.layerGroup().addTo(map);
  

  // 2. Add Cable Data to its group
  if (cable_data) {
    L.geoJSON(cable_data, {
      style: {
        color: 'grey',
        weight: 1,
        opacity: 0.7
      }
    }).addTo(cableLayer);
  }

  // 3. Loop and assign markers to groups
  status_data.locationCode.forEach((code, i) => {
    const isActive = status_data.any_active[i];
    const color = isActive ? "#28a745" : "#6c757d";
    const cleanName = status_data.locationName[i].replace(/Hydrophone [A-Z]/g, "Hydrophone Array");

    const deviceList = status_data.deviceCode[i].map((dev, idx) => {
      const status = status_data.end[i][idx] ? 
        '<span style="color:green;">Active</span>' : 
        '<span style="color:red;">Inactive</span>';
      return `<li><b>${dev}</b>: ${status}</li>`;
    }).join("");

    const marker = L.circleMarker([status_data.lat[i], status_data.lon[i]], {
      radius: 8,
      fillColor: color,
      color: "white",
      weight: 2,
      fillOpacity: 0.9
    })
    .bindPopup(`
      <div style="font-family: sans-serif; min-width: 200px;">
        <b style="font-size: 1.1em;">${cleanName}</b><br>
        <span style="color: #666; font-size: 0.9em;">${code}</span>
        <hr style="margin: 5px 0;">
        <p style="margin: 5px 0 0 0;"><b>Depth:</b> ${status_data.depth[i]} m</p>
        <p style="margin: 5px 0 0 0;"><b>Instruments at this site:</b></p>
        <ul style="margin: 5px 0; padding-left: 20px; font-size: 0.9em;">
          ${deviceList}
        </ul>
      </div>
    `);

    // Add marker to the appropriate group
    if (isActive) {
      marker.addTo(activeLayer);
    } else {
      marker.addTo(inactiveLayer);
    }
  });

  // 4. Create the Overlay Toggle Menu
  const overlays = {
    "<span style='color: #28a745; font-weight: bold;'>● Active Sites</span>": activeLayer,
    "<span style='color: #6c757d; font-weight: bold;'>○ Inactive Sites</span>": inactiveLayer,
    "Cables": cableLayer
  };

  // Add the control box to the top right
  L.control.layers(null, overlays, { collapsed: false }).addTo(map);
}

title: Hydrophone Array Design
text:
Challenges
When mounting hydrophones, the scientific goals of ocean monitoring will dictate how the infrasctructure is built. There exists several ways that hydrophones can be arranged depending on the application. For example, while a single hydrophone can capture sound, several hydrophones together can provide direction, range, and help with separation of overlapping noises.
At Ocean Networks Canada, we use two types of arrays:
The ultimate goal for acoustics data collection is to capture high-quality soundscape while reducing unwanted noise (high Signal to Noise ratio). Noise can originate from nearby instruments, cable vibration, debris hitting the sensor, or even strong ocean currents.
While it is no small feat (or rather impossible) to eliminate noise entirely, careful infrastructure design, engineering, and testing can drastically improve data quality. In addition, we always want to minimize the risk of damage to the instrument. This section touches on all these elements.

title: Hydrophone Array Design
text:
All possible configurations
Hydrophones can be deployed in several configurations:

Single Hydrophone: Baseline monitoring.
Line Arrays: Arranged in a straight line for vertical profiling.
USBL (Ultra-Short Base Line): Used for precise underwater positioning.
SBL (Short Baseline): Examples include 3-element horizontal arrays, 4-element tetrahedral arrays, or 4-element box-type arrays.
LBL (Long Base Line): Used for tracking over wide areas.

title: Hydrophone Array Design
text:
Interference
When adding hydrophones to a multi-instrument platform, all nearby infrastructure should ideally be evaluated for noise, although this is a hardeous task and we generally know after the facts if there is interference and to what internsity. Proximity interference will show up clearly as persistent lines or bursts in the spectrogram.
In this example from 2023, interference noise from instruments on the same platform at the Holyrood site was intense enough to fully mask natural ambient sound, biological vocalizations, and other signals of scientific interestes.



Example of instrument interference at Holyrood, Conception Bay, NL.


A full investigation for interference signal origin went on, in order to mitigate interference in future data. Brendan Smith at ONC did a full Interference Characterization in this Ocean Networks Canada: Holyrood Acoustic Testing Summary, and identified The suspected noises included the ADCP, pCO2 sensor, and Seabird Microcat. Following this investigation, …
To prevent acoustic contamination, the following “Rules of Thumb” separation distances between the hydrophone array and the systems:

Small Pumps (e.g., CTD pump): > 100 m
Large Pumps: > 2 km
Mooring chains > 5 km
Shackles/Hardware > 500 m
Active acoustics (e.g., ADCP) > 3 x the local water depth

Diagnosing interference before deployment: Acoustic modelling software (e.g., BELLHOP) can help diagnose potential interference if you know the source level, radiated frequencies, beam pattern, and beamwidth of neighboring instruments.

title: Hydrophone Array Design
text:
Infrastructure and flow noise
How a hydrophone is physically attached to the infrastructure makes a massive difference in data quality. Things like loose parts, rigid attachments, or using the wrong ties may affect data quality and show up as interference, or completely mask the data. Protective mounting hardwares are designed to minimize structural interference and flow noise.
Because hydrophones are pressure sensors, they are susceptible to pressure waves generated by surface swells (which decay exponentially with depth). Wave pressure can easily saturate the hydrophone’s dynamic range. Hydrophones should generally be moored at least 20 meters deep, and significantly deeper in areas known for large swells (e.g., Folger Passage).



Spectrograms for good vs bad mounting.


Here is a list of mounting material and their advantages or disadvantages:

Shock Cord/Bungee: Excellent (decouples vibration)
O-rings: Good (provides some dampening)
Rubber in a plastic clamp: Poor (transfers some vibration)
Rubber in a tight metal clamp: Bad (transfers heavy vibration)


The Shroud (mechanical protection)
Hydrophones are often protected by a cage (shroud), a semi-rigid housing that surrounds the hydrophone. The main purpose to prevent debris, sea life, or cables from striking the sensor. It can also help break turbulent flow (see Karman vortices) around the sensor, which can create low-frequency noise in the data.



Shrouds prevent debris, sea life, and more, from hitting the sensor.


Shrouds are acoustically transparent, designed so that they don’t reflect the sound waves.
In high sediments areas, platform parts can become covered by sediments which leads to corrosion and wear. Shrouds can prevent sediments to accumulate on hydrophone.

title: Hydrophone Array Design
text:
Biofouling
Biofouling is when marine life accumulates on the instrument. This can degrade the materials or significantly reduce data quality.
The “sock method”: we use a simple sock (graciously made by a local seamstress), which is a soft and flexible cover that fit snug over the hydrophone shroud. It greatly extends lifetime of all hydrophones by preventing microorganisms to directly attach on the sensor. It can also prevent fine sediments (e.g., silt) from settling into the small gaps.



The sock method prevents biofouling.


While no method is absolutely full proof, the sock-method has shown to be great success. Biofouling shows up in the data as sa slow decay of sensitivity over time.

title: Calibration (HydroCal)
text:
HydroCal
A hydrophone does not “hear” all frequencies equally. It might be increadibly sensitive at 10 kHz and deafen by 100 kHz, or vice-versa. This is why we have LF and HF hydrophones.
HydroCal User Manual
Ocean Networks Canada Hydrophone Calibration System, known as ONC HydroCal, is designed to perform two types of hydrophone calibrations: very low frequency (VLF) calibrations and high frequency (HF) calibrations.

title: Calibration (HydroCal)
text:
Ocean Sonics devices
Ocean Sonics calibrates their hydrophones at high frequencies (10 to 200 kHz), as well as at a single low-frequency point 26 Hz. HydroCal was developped to also calibrate ONC’s devices at low-frequencies (0.1 to 700 Hz), measure in-house by engineers at the MTC. The LF calibration is validated against Ocean Sonic’s 26 Hz.
The mid-range frequencies (700 Hz to 10 kHz) are not calibrated, but the curve is interpolated in between.

title: Calibration (HydroCal)
text:
Past issues
The ONC calibration curve was errounously calculated at very low frequencies. Therefore, the LF data seemed louder than it actually was. ONC is currently working towards implementing the new corrected calibration curve, and will have to re-generate all the previously archived data products.

title: Data Products
text:
Overview
Hydrophone data is archived several formats, ranging from raw acoustic recordings to processed spectral products.
Each device has a collection of files for every 5 minutes of recording:
Note that both Data Product Code and File Extension are needed when downloading data through the API. Each data product (dataProductCode) has a set of data product options (dpo) that can be use to narrow down the request.

title: Data Products
text:
Proprietary Spectral Data (.fft)
The Fast Fourier Transform files (.fft) are broadband spectrogram, and somewhat a raw version of the actual spectrogram. They are unique to Ocean Sonics hydrophones.
Technical warning: the .fft files are usually considered “preliminary” and not suitable for scientific research, because a single broadband sensitivity value (around -165 to -170 dB) was used rather than a ferquency-dependent calibration curve. They are best used for a “quick-look” to verify the hydrophone is functional.
Ocean Sonics has they in-house software Lucy to read the files, but it is also possible to read them with in python or MATLAB.

title: Data Products
text:
Processed Spectral Data (.mat)
The MATLAB files contain the true scientific data where the frequency-dependent sensitivity curve has been applied.

Calibration: Unlike .fft files, .mat spectral products are corrected using the specific hydrophone’s calibration data.
Structure: The data contain a SpectData MATLAB structure with frequency bins (Hz) and power spectral density.

title: Data Products
text:
Spectrogram (.png)
A spectrogram is a 2D heat map of sound, visualizing how the Power Spectral Density of an acoustic signal evolves over time.

X-axis: Time
Y-axis: Frequency (Hz)
Color/intensity: Sound Level expressed in \(\text{dB}~\text{re}~1~\mu Pa^2 / Hz\).

Here is how a spectrogram is generated:
To transform raw audio into a visual spectrogram, the data undergoes several steps:

Time Windowing: Raw audio is divided into short, finite segments using a time window (e.g., Hann Window), to prevent spectral leakage.
FFT Calculation: A Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) is applied to each segment, converting the signal from the time domain to the frequency domain.
Averaging: Successive windows are overlapped (typically by 50%) to ensure a smooth transition and prevent data loss at the window edges.
Calibration: The values are converted to physical units (\(\mu\) Pa) by applying the device-specific frequency-dependent calibration curve (this step is critical!).

[!IMPORTANT] There has been some issues in the past from using the wrong calibration curve: https://internal.oceannetworks.ca/spaces/ONCData/pages/255101693/1.+Power+spectral+density+vs.+power+spectrum.)
Why use Windows?
Fourier transforms assume a signal in infinite. By analyzing finite segments, at the edges of each segment there is a discontinuity that causes spectral leakage, where a signal energy leaks into neighboring frequency bins. Windowing (or tapering) solves this by multiplying the data segment by a specific mathematical shape (see examples below) that smooths the edges to zero. Mathematically, this is what is called a convolution. The Fourier transform of the convolution is the product of the individual Fourier transforms of the signal and the window.
The Stationary Assumption
Fourier transforms assume a signal is and stationary. The Heisenberg Uncertainty Principe dictates that you cannot have perfect resolution in both time and frequency simultaneously.

Narrow window: excellent time resolution (you see exactly when a whale click happened), but poor frequency resolution (the pitch is blurry).
Wide window: excellent frequency resolution (you see the exact harmonics of a whale call), but poor time resolution (start and end times are blurry).

Time windowing: Windowing is used to reduce spectral leakage when analyzing signals with a fourier transform. Multiplying a spectral segment by a window smooths the segment edges and recues discontinuities that cause leakage. Effectivemely, a segment is created by multiplying the data by a window of finitie length and a specific shape. This is called a convolution. The window that multiplies the function is called the window function (or sometimes tapering function). The Fourier transform of the convolution is the product of th eindividual Fourier transforms. There’s an implicit assumption that the signal frequency is invariant within the window, since it can only characterize stationay signals. Therefore, the signal is decomposed into finite segments (each segment is assumed stationary), and for each time window the Fourier transform is applied. If one slides the time-filtering window, then the signal is assumed to be decomposed with both information in frequency and time. Narrow window leads to goop time resolution, wide window leads to good frequency resolution. Can’t have both: Heisenberg Unertainty Principle. A drawback is that any signal with a wavelength longer than the window width is completely lost. In contrast, longer windows retain more frequency components, but at the expense of losing the time resolution of the signal.
I found a similar past issue here: DSO-598
Examples of windows:
In this example, the information of the spectrogram is as follows:

Hann window with 50% overlap at ONC
128000 samples per FFT window
Temporal resolution: 0.05 s
Spectral resolution: 10 Hz

title: Data Products
text:
Oceans3.0: Hydrophone Auxiliary Data
Ocean Sonics devices provide a .txt file with auxiliary data, which are all the data related to the instrument itself, not the passive acoustics. For example, information about internal humidity, temperature, battery charge, etc., are contained within this product. Although the auxiliary data does not give any information regarding the ocean soudscape, it can be useful to diagnose potential issues, such as water leakage within the hydrophone (>50% humidity), overheating, or dead sensor (dead battery). There are many more variables like magnetic flux and acceleration which can be used to determine the hydrophone direction and strong currents.
More information here: [insert link].

title: Data Diversion
text:
https://wiki.oceannetworks.ca/spaces/DP/pages/42174740/Diversion+of+Tier+1+data
Underwater acoustics data may reveal sensitive information regarding national defense. Therefore, several hydrophone arrays are equipped with a “driver” in which there is a switch which allows for data diversion.
Data diversion means the recorded and stored data is not streamed to Ocean Networks Canada archives, but is rather diverted to other instances, such as the Navy or the Departrment of National Defense.oceannetworks During diversion, at ONC, we annotated DDS (Data Diversion System). We should receive an email during planned diversion. Often, the data will come back to use either untoucher or low-passed filtered (cutoff usually around ???). In case of low-passed filtered, the data is then archived with the suffix LPF (ex: ICLISTENHF6016-20260423-…-LPF.flac). The .fft format is however not LPF, but it is not possible to recover the spectrogram from fft only since .fft does not contain the phase information (to verifiy).
“Hydrophones at ONC are categorized Tier1 instruments (instruments that are of concern to the Royal Canadian Navy (RCN) and US Navy (USN)). Therefore, the raw acoustic data may be subject to diversion before they are returned to the ONC archive.”
from DSO confluence page:
Diversions Some notes about Data Diversion (DDS) from Trident Data Service by Dave regarding High Security devices: Normally data is “bypassed” directly to ONC (with no diversion), but there are times when the data is “diverted” to a US Navy screening facility to allow for processing to determine if the data is too sensitive for public release. The DDS’ primary role is to allow for data “screening” during times specified by the Canadian and US Navies. After screening, the data is either released or withheld, depending on sensitivity. The screening process occurs on a file basis. After screening, the shorter the file size, the more data that is potentially released. These divert periods are controlled by the Canadian and US Navies. They may happen at any time and may last for minutes to days. Whenever there is a diversion mode change, the current files are stopped, and new files are generated. This method optimizes the amount of data released. Example (assuming files are 1/2 hour long) New file starts 10:00:00. At 10:11:37 the DDS is commanded to enter a divert mode. There will be a “bypass” file from 10:00:00 to 10:11:36, with a new “divert” file starting at 10:11:37. Another new file will then begin at 10:30:00, closing the file from 10:11:37 to 10:29:59. This and subsequent files will be diverted until the mode changes, and the reverse happens. Audio data (WAV, FLAC) files may be subject to military diversion. Summary page for external users - http://wiki.neptunecanada.ca/display/DP/Diversion+of+Hydrophone+and+Seismometer+data. The military doesn’t give us filtered data anymore. We either receive full band data back or none at all if it’s sensitive. There is an e-mail list (divert@lists.uvic.ca), but only people with appropriate security clearance (ie. not hydrophone data stewards) are added to this list. See Tools Admin#Emailgroups

No audio data stream -> Junction Box down, network down

is it related to the instrument itself/data quality? INSTR ticket

title: Hydrophone Specs & Manufacturers
text:
Information about suppliers and manufacturers
Ocean Networks Canada uses hydrophones from several manufacturers and distributors: the main ones being Ocean Sonics and JASCO Applied Sciences.
Most hydrophones are Ocean Sonics icListen smart hydrophones: icListen HF (High Frequency; 10 Hz to 200k Hz), and icListen AF (Audio Frequency; 10 Hz to 12 kHz). JASCO provides Geospectrum-manufactured hydrophones for multi-element directional arrays, great for localizing sources.

title: Hydrophone Specs & Manufacturers
text:
JASCO Applied Sciences {{< fa link >}}
A scientific consulting firm and equipment supplier specializing in underwater acoustics. Founded by Joseph Arnold Scrimger in Victoria, British Columbia, in 1981, the company now has its largest primary operations office in Halifax, Nova Scotia, Canada.
JASCO operates globally across national defense, marine environmental stewardship, oil and gas asset monitoring, and academic research. They also perform acoustic modeling, field data collection, and advanced analysis suites.
Their Autonomous Multichannel Acoustic Recorder (AMAR) provides passive acoustic data acquisition with high bandwidth and low noise.
Their hydrophones are manufactured by GeoSpectrum Technologies Inc.

title: Hydrophone Specs & Manufacturers
text:
GeoSpectrum Technologies Inc. (GTI) {{< fa link >}}
A specialized Canadian aerospace and defense company, based in Halifax, NS, that focuses on the design, engineering, and manufacturing of underwater acoustic transducers and specialized systems. GeoSpectrum hydrophones are high-performance, and frequently deployed as part of hydrophone arrays managed by JASCO.

title: Hydrophone Specs & Manufacturers
text:
Ocean Sonics {{< fa link >}}
Ocean Sonics is a canadian, Nova Scotia-based pioneer in the development of smart digital hydrophones, found in 2005. They have developed both low-frequency (icListenLF/AF) and high-frequency (icListenHF) hydrophones.

title: Glossary
section: Definition of acousitc metrics and terms
text:
Definition of acousitc metrics and terms

Absorption
Reduction of acoustic pressure amplitude due to acoustic particle motion energy converting to heat in the propagation medium.


Ambiant sound/noise
Sound that would be present in the absence of a specified activity. It is location-specific and time-specific. def 2: all-encompassing sound at a given place, usually a composite of sound from many sources near and far (e.g. shipping vessels, seismic activity, precipitation, sea ice, wave, biological activity).


Attenuation
Gradual loss of acoustic energy from absorption and scattering as sound propagates through a medium.


Background noise
All sound recorded by the hydrophone in the absence of the pile driving signal for a specified pile driving acoustic signal beaing measured. ### Propagation loss


Cetacean
Any anumal in the order Cetacea. These are aquatic, mostly maine mammals and include whales, dolphins, and porpoises.


Compressional wave
A mechanical vibration wave in which the direction of particle motion is parallel to the direction of the propagation. Also called primary wave, or P-wave.


Decibel (dB)
Relative unit of measurement equal to one-tenth of a bel, measuring the level when the base of the logarithm is the tenth root of ten, and the quantities concerned are proportional to power.


Ensonified
Exposed to sound.


Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)
A computationally efficiently algorithm for computing the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT). A DFT requires \(O(n^2)\) computations, while an FFT requires \(O(n log n)\). A Fourier transform converts a signal from its original domain (time/space) to a representation in the frequency domain.


Frequency
The rate of oscillation of a periodic function measured in cycles-per-unit-time. Symbol: \(f\). Unit: Hertz (Hz), is equal to 1 cycle per second.


Intensity
The intensity (\(I\)) of a sound wave is defined as the energy transmitted through a unit area, per unit time, in the direction in which the sound wave is traveling. It is a vector quantity having both a magnitude and direction.
\[
\vec{I} = p \vec{u}
\]
where \(p\) is pressure, and \(u\) the particle velocity.


Impedance
Impedance of water is much greater than that of air.


Power Spectrum Density
The acoustic signal power per unit frequency as measured at a single frequency. Unit: \(\micro Pa^2\cdot s\)


Pressure
Unit: Pa

acoustic: deviation from the ambient hydrostatic pressure caused by a sound wave.
hydrostatic: pressure at any given depth in a static liquid that is the result of the weight of the liquid acting on a unit area at that depth, plus any pressure acting on the surface of the liquid.



Reverberation
Sound resulting from cumulative scattering of sound by an aggregation, or ensemble, of scatterers.


Root-mean-square sound pressure level (SPL) \(L_{p,rms}\)
The Root Mean Square (RMS) of the sound pressure taken over a time interval \(\Delta t = t_2-t_1\) [s]. Starting from the Mean Square average sound pressure \(p_{ms}\), [Pa$^2] the RMS pressure \(p_{rms}\) [Pa] is:
\[
p_{ms} = \bar p^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta t} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} p^2 (t) dt
\]
… there is more…


Soundscape
Underwater acoustics characterization of the ambient sound in terms of its spatial, temporal and frequency attributes, and the types of sources contributing to the sound field. ### Pulse duration The pulse duration is the percentage energy signal duration over the acoustic pulse, defined in ISO 18406, assuming an energy percentage for the pulse duration of 90%.


Sound pressure (\(p\))
Contribution to total pressure caused by the action of sound. Sound pressure is a function of time: \(p=p(t)\), and expressed in pascals (Pa).


Sound pressure spectrum (\(P\))
Fourier transform of the sound pressure. Sound pressure spectrum is a function of frequency: \(P=P(f)\), and expressed in pascals per hertz (Pa Hz-1). The formula form is:
\[
P(f) = \int_\infty^\infty exp\left(-2\pi i f t \right) p(t) dt
\]


Sound Exposure Level (SEL)


Source level (SL\(_E\))


Sound speed profile
Speed of sound in the water column as a function of depth below the water surface.


Spectrogram
A visual representation of acoustic amplitude compared with time and frequency.


Transmission loss


Wavelength
Distance over which a wave completes one scillation cycle. Unit: meter (m). Symbol: \(\lambda\).
